page_content:
Chapter 45
Performance Basics
Performance is a feature. Slow pages lose users, lower search rankings, and drain mobile
batteries. A handful of techniques get you most of the way.
Measure first
Use Lighthouse and the WebPageTest service to measure real page performance. The metrics
that matter most for users are Largest Contentful Paint, Interaction to Next Paint, and Cumulative
Layout Shift, collectively known as Core Web Vitals.
Ship less code
The single biggest win for most sites is shipping less JavaScript. Audit your bundle with a tool
like source-map-explorer. Remove unused dependencies. Prefer the platform (dialog, popover,
details) over JavaScript replacements.
Images
Images are usually the largest assets on a page. Serve modern formats (AVIF, WebP), include
srcset for responsive sizing, lazy-load below the fold, and always specify width and height to
prevent layout shift.
Caching
Set far-future cache headers on assets with hashed filenames. The first visit is slow; every

page_content:
rendering.
Performance
Record a profile while reproducing a slow interaction. The resulting flame chart shows exactly
which functions, layouts, and paints took how long. Most performance problems become obvious
after one good profile.
Application
Inspect localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, IndexedDB, cache storage, and service workers.
Clear them all when debugging weird stale-data behavior. The Service Workers section is
essential for any offline-capable app.

page_content:
prevent layout shift.
Caching
Set far-future cache headers on assets with hashed filenames. The first visit is slow; every
subsequent visit and every navigation within the site reuses cached assets. A good CDN turns
global latency into local latency.
Avoid layout thrashing
Reading layout properties (offsetTop, getBoundingClientRect) after writing to the DOM forces the
browser to recalculate layout synchronously. Batch reads and writes, or use
requestAnimationFrame for animation work.
